author: @npalen
text:
I ask this question with respect to Robert's legacy and only wish that he was still with us to answer it:  
I'm wondering if the four slotted planetary gears will have to be phased differently in order to operate the "output ratchets " in sequence.  With each of the four slots pointing in the same relative direction with respect to centerline, would they not all be trying to "ratchet" at the same time rather than in sequence throughout each rotation of the carrier?  Help me understand, please.

author: @jimgraham6722
text:
Thanks Robert for the explanation, demo and 3D print.

This is an ingenious planetry cvt. As I understand it is a fairly recent mechanical concept, notable for its high level of transmission efficiency. In time it will likely be perfected and wind up in all sorts of applications. Not just motor vehicles but also anywhere constant rpm needed such as AC alternators under variable load or for example variable speed wind turbines driving a constant speed alternator. Previous CVTs have had poor efficiency and were prone to wear and tear. There are endless possibilities for a better device.

replies.author: @danv2888
replies.text: It really does still need to be perfected. From what I have heard it still does not provide a continuous output. If you think of a continuous output as a straight line. It would would work more like a sine wave. So there is still some in-efficiency. However, it is still the best possible geared CVT that people have come up with.

replies.author: @FilipMikulic-u7d

replies.author: @kadmow
replies.text: -   in a  motor vehicle with surplus power a 2HP  power cost isn't a big deal, while in a bicycle the same power cost is "prohibitively expensive" - on a "power available" cost function.  Series electrics at low (rotational speed) power inputs are rather "lossy" - actually series electric under nearly any schema isn't anywhere as efficient as a good gear train (infinite variability - without power surging and oscillating  parts is the holy grail). For example: Edison motors (battery augmented series  diesel electric) and diesel electric trains (ie. NOT a hybrid) are only "better" in their chosen operating cycle because distributed power (huge capacities) and a lower average power input van result in lower total fuel burn - the ability to store downhill "retardation" to use on the next uphill has appeal.  Long distance trucking afaik hasn't given back significant savings from diesel-electric - optimising a split-duty diesel engine would probably do more for reducing fuel burn.

author: @lostvisitor
text:
Always love your videos.

I have a request.  

I was working to improve the cvt on my scooter.  It has a pully in the front that changes the ratio of a belt as the rpm of the engine increases and a clutch that is engaged by the speed of the wheels.  I hope you see the flaw in this design.  Some one came up with it years ago and every  one just ran with it.

What should happen is that the engine should spin up and engage the clutch and the speed of the wheel controls the gear.  This way the clutch is fully engaged at all times and the load at the wheels shifts the gears.
To do this the clutch moves forward with the torque spring still trying to force the plates together and the clutch drum will spin around to be on the inside and attached to the outer pully plate.  The drive side will become a little more complex with a clutch spring holding the plates together and weighted swing arms that will pull the plates apart as the drive side increases in speed. The hard part will be to find the right balance between the force of the front and rear pulleys.  But once the balance is found: One design will work no matter what is modified to the engine and tires.

I do not have access to 3d printer or any of the other stuff I would need to design this properly but I bet you could just have a grand old time with it.

I'm not interested in making money off of this just wanting things to work better.  This is why I like your channel.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I think that's a centrifugal clutch mate - but in a centrifugal clutch the input is connected to the engine. It has to be that way round or you would never get going.

author: @RePeteAndMe
text: Cool. I like the RatioZero CVT. My engine's Displacement Adjuster has perhaps nine re-expansion cylinders and three pre-compression cylinders so the expansion to compression ratio is three at any torque. Ratio zeros CVT links the DA's crankshaft to the engine's crankshaft. The faster the DA's speed relative to the engine the more torque is produced. No throttle or turbo required.

author: @larsborstlap3461
text: Love the enthusiasm and the designs! Please correct me if I am wrong, I just want to learn. But this is not the ratio-zero concept and this design does not work. You need one-way clutches in the 4 black gears at the end of the yellow arms (this is what ratio-zero does). Otherwise they would just spin without being able to transfer torque . Also in the Ratio-Zero device the planet carrier is stationary compared to the ground. The planet wheels do spin around there axis. and Ratio-Zero uses the ring gear as the input.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I don't think you are right mate - both in the patent and in demo models I have seen of this - there is no one way clutch. I guess you could use them if you wanted - it would make it unidirectional - no reverse - but you don't need them - torque transfer is not by the spinning of the gears but rather the 'pull' of the lever - besides you saw it work in this video. Equally it doesn't matter in a planetary gear system what is used as input - as the planets are taken as output either the ring or sun is fine as input as the motion of the system is within that frame of reference which makes the planet carrier stationary in relation

replies.author: @virenheera7374
replies.text: Really appreciate the build and ingenuity. I'm also trying to understand how the arm can "pull" or exert any torque on the output gear if the small gears (at the end of the arms) are just free-wheeling. As an experiment, it would be interesting to see if the model can transfer some torque, maybe by applying some slight resistance on the output gear as you turn the input gear (while holding the outer ring gear stationary). Thanks.

replies.author: @herbys68
replies.text:
@virenheera7374 I'm struggling with the same issue, and in the print I made, it doesn't work precisely because of that: the output gears move alright, but they don't pull on the output gear because of two reasons: one, nothing keeps them pressed out to the inside of the output ring, and second the output gears spin freely, so they don't exert any force on the geared output ring. 
I think both issues are related to me missing a piece, probably a gear around the shaft that presses the output gears out and also syncs their spin, but I don't see it in the video and I am not sure that's the correct answer.  @ThinkingandTinkering, can you clarify what goes between the output gears that keeps them pressed outwards so they make contact with the output ring?

author: @williampeters2046
text: A better solution is the one Toyota used in the prious, where the hand crank on your model connected to a generator, and the gas engine connected to the planet gears.  The outside of the ring gear connects to an electric motor and the rest of the drive train.  If the generator is turned off, the engine cannot transmit power to the drive train.  The electric motor can directly drive the output without the gas engine running.  By varying the load on the central generator, the effective gear ratio is then varied. or the central generator can be placed in motor mode and add more electric power to the output.  Its very simplel and robust.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @jamesross1003
text: This is brilliant!  Caveats of course, but taking into account how robust it is built would solve a lot of issues(not all but most,  a secondary braking would probably be needed as ramping up this is brilliant but braking or ramping down ratio would quickly damage gearing).  Like everything it depends on end use of the mechanism.  It reminds me a lot of a ringing circuit in electronics that has variable inductance and variable capacitance.  Surprisingly enough even the waste heat is similar in active function of both circuit and this cvt.  In said circuit you would just need a resistor that is up to the task, so add a resistance of some sort to the input when braking is needed should work.  Over all, a brilliant design and beautifully done Robert!  Thanks for the video!

author: @JaenFoster
text: If you want to know why CVTs aren't ubiquitous, blame Formula 1. Williams trialed a CVT in the mid 90's and the powers that be decided it provided an unfair advantage and banned it. Had they not done so, most teams would have likely put a lot of R&D into them to find ways to overcome their disadvantages.

replies.text: @WarblesWarkly the variomatic CVT was patented in 1898!

replies.author: @raven_talionis
replies.text: ​@mandrakejake  did you mean 1898 or 1998? Just asking for clarification due to a earlier comment saying "mid 90's"

replies.author: @lostvisitor
replies.text: If you want a vehicle to last don't get a CVT.  They are only good for about 100K miles if you are NICE to them.  They are used dominantly in new scooter, atvs, and utv.  The belt is a consumable part and only lasts about a year.  There use has NOTHING to do with fuel economy or performance.  It is all about cost and planed obsolesce.  (Corporate  Profits)

replies.author: @mandrakejake
replies.text: @raven_talionis as I said 1898, it's on Wikipedia.
